profile.name: Pardhu Sri Rushi Varma Konduru
profile.bio: We present the author — a final-year cybersecurity undergraduate and independent researcher operating out of Hyderabad, India. The subject exhibits a persistent tendency to disassemble working systems in order to understand why they work: kernels, package managers, and machine-learning models alike. Current research examines how learned systems behave after deployment — how they adapt, drift, and occasionally surprise their creators. Secondary findings include a fondness for the Unix philosophy, keyboard-driven workflows, and Arch Linux. The author remains, at time of writing, merely a vessel of a greater curiosity.
profile.title: Curiosity-Driven Research in Machine Learning & Systems Security

about.title: Introduction
paragraphs: The author's work sits at the intersection of ==machine learning reliability== and ==low-level systems security==. Most days are spent reading research papers, building in Rust or Go, or tweaking an Arch Linux setup. The underlying hypothesis, held since childhood, is that the world is open source: everything can be read, understood, and — with one more tweak — improved.

This curiosity has produced kernel-level defense frameworks, a package manager, hardware security platforms, and two research manuscripts. It has also produced a strong preference for ==software simple enough to understand yet powerful enough to grow with its users==.

research.title: Related & Ongoing Work
paragraphs: The author's first preprint [1] explores the structural limitations of weight-based neural adaptation—what a network can and cannot become by adjusting weights alone. A second manuscript, on the temporal and adaptive dynamics of latent behavioral state machines, is currently in preparation.

Research interests wander comfortably between machine learning and low-level systems. Some days that means thinking about adaptive learning dynamics and AI safety; other days it means reading kernel source code, experimenting with Linux internals, or building developer tools in Rust and Go. The constant has never really been a field—it's curiosity. If a system is interesting, there's a good chance it ends with reading the source code, asking too many questions, and wondering how it could be made just a little better.

systems.name: kernel-borderlands
systems.description: Kernel-level runtime defense: detects and contains anomalous process behavior at Ring 0 on Linux.

systems.name: emrac
systems.description: Source-first package management platform for the Arch Linux ecosystem.

systems.name: Project Ouroboros
systems.description: Dual-use ESP32 hardware/firmware platform for wireless defense, monitoring, and research.

systems.name: lbsm-research
systems.description: Latent Behavioral State Machines and statistical manifolds.

systems.name: rlae-research
systems.description: Adaptive learning and emergence — reversibility in learned systems.

certs.name: API Security Fundamentals + OWASP API Top 10

ctfParagraphs: As Technical Challenge Author & Cloud Deployment Operations (Feb–Mar 2026), the author designed challenges spanning ==binary exploitation, cryptography, reverse engineering, blockchain, mobile security, and steganography== for a national-level student-led CTF run with the National Cyber Security Research Council and EC-Council — implemented across Python, C, C++, Rust, Zig, Go, Kotlin, Solidity, and x86 assembly — and architected the containerized infrastructure for reproducible live deployment.
In parallel evaluation environments, the author holds ==LEGEND rank on TryHackMe — top 2% globally== [3], with ongoing practice tracks on Hack The Box.

references.text: P. S. R. V. Konduru, “On the Structural Limitations of Weight-Based Neural Adaptation,”
